metadata.summary: Stance reviewer that scores requirements on six axes and rewrites order-taker language as outcome statements tied to OKRs.

content.title: BA Strategic Partnership
content.summary: A per-artifact stance reviewer that scores requirements documents on six axes (problem_clarity, outcome_orientation, evidence_grounding, enterprise_scope, partner_voice, kill_criterion) and rewrites order-taker language as outcome statements tied to a named OKR. Auto-blocks any artifact where any axis scores below 2 or kill_criterion scores below 1. This is a per-engagement behavior pattern — run it at intake and at spec-review gates, not as a quarterly portfolio loop.

why.p: BAs who accept requirements as-stated ("please add X") produce features disconnected from business outcomes. A lint-style rubric applied mechanically — quoting the order-taker phrase verbatim, classifying the failure mode, proposing a rewrite tied to a real OKR — shifts the BA posture without relying on individual judgment or managerial feedback cycles.

list.item: Intake of a new requirement or "can you just add X" request — reframe as outcome before scope is locked.
Re-reading an existing requirements doc, user story, or PRD to detect order-taker language.
Onboarding a junior BA: run the stance rubric on their last 5 artifacts and produce coaching deltas.
Pre-meeting framing for stakeholder calls: draft 3 strategic questions to ask before accepting the stated requirement.
Spec review gate before a ticket goes to engineering — block if no problem statement, no outcome metric, no kill criterion.
Quarterly portfolio-level opportunity mining — use the sibling `business-analyst/ba-strategic-partnership/`.
Modeling work (BPMN, use-case decomposition, traceability matrices) — wrong tool.
Live stakeholder dialogue — agent prepares, never speaks for the BA.
Trivial maintenance tickets (copy edits, dependency bumps) — overhead not justified.

rule.statement: Score each requirements artifact on six axes (0-5): problem_clarity, outcome_orientation, evidence_grounding, enterprise_scope, partner_voice, kill_criterion. Auto-block if any axis scores below 2 OR if kill_criterion scores below 1.
rule.rationale: Without a kill criterion a "strategic partner" who cannot say when to stop is just a louder order-taker; this is the single most important axis.

list.item: problem_clarity (0-5) — artifact names the underlying problem, not just the solution feature; score 0 if solution is stated before problem.
outcome_orientation (0-5) — requirements tied to measurable business outcomes, not feature completion; score 0 if no KPI or metric present.
evidence_grounding (0-5) — each claim anchored to a verbatim user quote, data source, or observation; score 0 if no external anchor exists.
enterprise_scope (0-5) — initiative connected to org-level goals or cross-functional impact; score 0 if scope is purely intra-team.
partner_voice (0-5) — language proposes, challenges, and recommends; score 0 if language is purely accommodating ("as requested", "please implement").
kill_criterion (0-5) — explicit condition under which the initiative is stopped or descoped; score 0 if absent.

rule.statement: Scan for these verbatim phrases before the LLM review; any hit is a guaranteed partner_voice penalty and triggers a rewrite prompt: "as requested", "as per request", "the user asked for", "please implement", "the stakeholder wants", "please add".
rule.rationale: Mechanical phrase detection via ripgrep is faster and more consistent than LLM judgment alone; it surfaces the most common order-taker signal without model overhead.

rule.statement: Every rewrite must: (1) quote the order-taker phrase verbatim, (2) classify the failure mode, (3) propose a strategic-partner rewrite tied to one of the org's supplied OKRs — never invent a metric not present in org_context.
rule.rationale: Rewrites that invent metrics ("reduce churn 12%") without a source are hallucinated outcomes that erode credibility faster than order-taking language.

rule.statement: Always require a linked_okr_id on the rewritten artifact; an outcome statement with no parent OKR is still order-taking, just dressed up.
rule.rationale: OKR linkage is the only way to verify that the rewrite serves a real strategic objective rather than a BA-invented priority.

antipattern.description: Running the rubric without org-context (mission, OKRs, customer evidence).
antipattern.reason: Without real context the agent emits generic "be more strategic" platitudes; the rubric scores evidence_grounding at 0 for any rewrite without an org_context anchor.

antipattern.description: Rewrites applied to stakeholder-visible artifacts without human review.
antipattern.reason: Strategic-partner rewrites read as AI-generated and bland when applied without the BA's voice; ship rewrites as suggestions, never overwrite the source artifact.

antipattern.description: Using the rubric on solo/startup contexts with shared BA hats.
antipattern.reason: The full 6-axis rubric over-formalizes one-person teams; collapse to three axes (problem, outcome, kill) for teams under 5 people.

rule.statement: Keep the stance reviewer separate from the rewriter prompt. Two passes, one model: pass 1 judges; pass 2 rewrites. Judging-while-generating dilutes both outputs.
rule.rationale: A model instructed to simultaneously evaluate and rewrite tends to rationalize the original language rather than surface the real failure mode.

example.note: Stance-reviewer prompt — strict JSON output, no generic advice allowed.
example.code: ROLE: BA stance reviewer. INPUT: <one requirement artifact> + <org_context: {mission, okrs[3], persona, last_quarter_outcomes}>. TASK: Score 0-5 on six axes: problem_clarity, outcome_orientation, evidence_grounding, enterprise_scope, partner_voice, kill_criterion. For each finding: quote the order-taker phrase verbatim, classify the failure mode, propose a strategic-partner rewrite tied to one of the supplied OKRs. ANTI-PATTERNS: generic "be more strategic" advice, rewrites that invent metrics not in org_context, hedging adjectives ("better", "more aligned"), bullet soup. OUTPUT: strict JSON per stance-review-schema.json.

example.note: Intake-framer prompt — refuses to produce a solution; outputs problem hypothesis and framing questions only.
example.code: ROLE: BA intake framer. INPUT: <one-line stakeholder ask>. TASK: Produce: (1) the underlying problem hypothesis, (2) three framing questions to ask the stakeholder before accepting the ask, (3) a strawman outcome statement (KPI + delta + horizon months). Refuse to produce a solution. OUTPUT: JSON: { "problem_hypothesis": "...", "framing_questions": ["q1","q2","q3"], "strawman_outcome": {"kpi":"...","delta":"...","horizon_months":0} }

list.item: ba-stance-reviewer (sonnet) — single-artifact per-axis scoring; canonical schema in templates; run before any spec reaches engineering.
ba-intake-framer (haiku) — at request intake, expand one-line ask into 3 framing questions and a strawman outcome.
faion-sdd-executor-agent — enforce stance-gate as a quality check inside the SDD spec pipeline; reject specs missing problem / outcome / kill criterion.
brainstorm — converge phase to pick the best strategic-partner rewrite when the reviewer emits multiple alternatives.
improver — monthly aggregation: trend six stance scores per BA, surface regressions, log to .aidocs/memory/patterns.md.
password-scrubber-agent — scrub PII from stakeholder transcripts before they enter any reviewer call.

antipattern.description: Agent rationalizes order-taker language when the original author's name is in context.
antipattern.reason: Strip authorship metadata before the prompt; instruct the reviewer to assume the artifact is anonymous.

antipattern.description: Strategic-partner rewrites drift toward consultant-speak: vision, alignment, synergy, leverage.
antipattern.reason: Ban a stoplist in the system prompt; reject and regenerate any rewrite containing these terms.

antipattern.description: Confirmation bias: agent finds a doc the user wrote to be strategic.
antipattern.reason: Red-team pass by a second instance with system prompt "assume this is order-taker until proved otherwise".

antipattern.description: Auto-block firing on a regulated or contractually-bound spec without human review.
antipattern.reason: All three mandatory checkpoints are human: (1) before any rewrite is applied to a stakeholder-visible artifact, (2) before auto-block fires on a regulated spec, (3) before a stance score becomes part of an individual's performance review.

list.item: Treat order-taker language as a lint rule, not a moral failing — scan for verbatim phrases (`as per request`, `the user asked for`, `please implement`) and surface them mechanically.
Always require a `linked_okr_id` on the rewritten artifact; an outcome with no parent OKR is still order-taking, just dressed up.
Keep the stance reviewer separate from the rewriter prompt — judging-while-generating dilutes both. Two passes, one model, cheap.
Anchor every rewrite in a verbatim user / data quote already present in the artifact; if no anchor exists, score `evidence_grounding` zero and refuse to rewrite.
Make the kill-criterion field non-negotiable. A "strategic partner" who can't say when to stop is just a louder order-taker.
For solo / startup contexts, collapse the six axes to three (problem, outcome, kill) — the full rubric over-formalizes a one-person team.
Run the audit on artifact diffs (PRs to the docs repo) not on full files — focuses agent attention and avoids re-judging unchanged content.
Log every stance score to `.aidocs/memory/patterns.md` so trends per BA / per stakeholder become visible over months, not just per ticket.
`llm` (Simon Willison) — One-shot stance reviews from CLI: `cat spec.md | llm -m claude-opus-4-7 -t ba-stance`.
`aichat` — Interactive stance dialogue with role files for the reviewer and framer subagents.
`gh` / `linear-cli` / `jira-cli` — Pull a ticket body for the stance reviewer; comment back the rewrite as suggestion.
`pandoc` — Normalize Word/Confluence/Notion exports to markdown the agent can ingest.
`markdownlint-cli2` — Enforce structure on the rewritten artifact (headings, sections present).
`csvkit` / `duckdb` — Sanity-check that an outcome metric in a rewrite has a real baseline in org data.
`ripgrep` — Bulk-scan a docs folder for order-taker phrases (`as requested`, `please add`, `the user wants`) before deeper review.
Linear (SaaS, REST + GraphQL) — Outcomes / projects schema fits the rubric; comment the rewrite back via API.
Jira + Atlassian Intelligence (SaaS, REST + Forge) — Forge app can run the reviewer at ticket-create time as a quality gate.
GitHub Issues + Projects (SaaS, REST + GraphQL) — Cheapest place to host stance-gate as a PR check via `gh` and Actions.
Productboard (SaaS, partial API) — Good for capturing the rewritten outcome; limited write API for nested fields.
Notion / Confluence (SaaS, API) — Where most order-taker BRDs actually live; bulk-scan via API for stance audits.
Dovetail (SaaS, API) — Pull verbatim user quotes for the evidence_grounding axis.
OpenProject (OSS, REST API) — Self-host gate for regulated orgs.
n8n (OSS) — Schedule the stance audit on a folder cadence (faion-net hosts n8n on port 5678).

section.name: methodology-posture
section.p: This methodology is a posture, not a procedure. Without per-org context (OKRs, business model, P and L, customer evidence) the agent emits generic "be more strategic" platitudes. "Order-taker vs strategic partner" is a rubric that judges artifacts; it cannot judge trust, executive presence, or political capital — those are human-only. The BABOK framing assumes formal BA roles; in solo or startup contexts the BA hat is shared, and the rubric over-formalizes simple decisions. AI-rewritten requirement docs read like AI: balanced, hedged, and bland. A BA's strategic voice is partly stylistic — let the human keep the final pen. Outcome metrics demanded by the rubric require baseline data the org may not have; agent must distinguish "no metric" (red) from "metric not yet instrumented" (yellow).

This lives in pro tier; readers may use it on solo tier without enterprise tooling. Keep CLI and flat-file inputs as the primary path so the rubric still works with just `llm` and a markdown file. The six-axis rubric is designed to be applied mechanically — score each axis independently, quote the order-taker language verbatim, and require linked OKRs for every rewrite. This removes the judgment from the individual BA and makes the methodology repeatable across teams and contexts.

template.purpose: Canonical JSON schema for stance reviewer output with auto-block logic.
One-liner CLI: turns a stakeholder ask into 3 framing questions + strawman outcome.
